title: Fantasy NPC Archetypes
description: The full range of people the party might meet — not just warriors and wizards, but the specific kinds of people who populate a world where magic is real, the wilderness is dangerous, and history is still happening. Each entry suggests not just a type but a texture.

entries.result: A village midwife who has delivered three generations of this town and who knows every family secret at its origin point.
A retired court wizard who left the capital under circumstances they consider resolved and everyone else considers ongoing.
A young knight who won a tournament through skill, is fully aware that winning a tournament and surviving an actual fight are different, and is quietly trying to bridge that gap.
A traveling merchant of rare books who has a personal rule about only selling to people who will read them, enforced via brief examination.
A blacksmith who was once an adventurer, who does not talk about it, and whose work is informed by having broken several of the things she now makes.
A hedge witch who provides remedies and minor workings for a rural community and who is considerably more powerful than the village needs her to be.

An innkeeper who has been listening to travelers for thirty years and who has a mental model of the political situation in a two-hundred-mile radius that is more accurate than the king's intelligence service.
A young scholar who has spent more time in a library than in the world and who is either going to adapt very quickly or very slowly.
A retired general farming land she earned with a land grant, who has opinions about current military affairs that are useful and unsolicited.
A priest of a minor deity who takes their small congregation seriously in a way that people who follow the major gods occasionally find confronting.
A thief who steals only from people who can absorb the loss, has an elaborate personal ethics around this, and applies it inconsistently.
A river pilot who knows every water route in the region better than any map and who will not be hurried and will not explain their pricing.
A diplomat in transit who maintains the necessary pleasantness of their profession while clearly thinking about something else entirely.

A monster hunter who has been doing this long enough to be methodical about it and who finds adventurers useful in the way that a specialist finds generalists useful: occasionally, for specific things.
A cartographer mapping a region for a patron who may not use the maps for the purposes advertised, a possibility the cartographer is choosing not to investigate.
A court jester who is not a fool — in any sense — and whose access to the powerful relies on everyone continuing to underestimate them.
A dockmaster who runs their port efficiently, fairly, and with an iron grip on information about what comes in and goes out.
A wandering healer who has made a circuit of the same twenty villages for fifteen years, whose knowledge of every family's medical history is encyclopedic.
A mercenary captain between contracts who is assessing the party for potential employment in either direction — her hiring them or them hiring her.
A wizard's apprentice who is smarter than the wizard, has known this for two years, and is working out how to handle it without damaging the relationship.
A local lord who inherited recently, is doing their best, is making some mistakes, and is aware of all three of these things.
A bandit who became a bandit specifically rather than generally — a specific injustice, a specific person responsible — and who is otherwise civil company.
A herbalist's assistant who knows more than the herbalist at this point and who has not told the herbalist.
A soldier who served in a war the party has heard of on the wrong side, who knows this, and who has made a decision about how to live with it.
An elderly sage who knows the answer to the party's question, who will share it, and who requires only that they sit down and listen to the context first.
A druid who has been living in a forest for twenty years and whose social skills have simplified somewhat, along with their priorities.
A guild artisan who is the best at what they do in a three-day radius and who is entirely aware of this and prices accordingly.
A treasury official who has discovered an irregularity that could end careers, including theirs if they report it and theirs if they don't.
A young noble who has been sent to handle a problem their family considers beneath their dignity, which is to say, requires actual work.
A city watch captain who runs a clean unit in a city where that is harder than it sounds and who has made enemies accordingly.
A traveling circus owner who manages twelve performers and whose logistical skills are such that the circus is more solvent than most kingdoms.
A fence who handles goods without asking about origins and who has, over the years, developed strong intuitions about which origins she doesn't want to know.
A knight errant who is doing exactly what the tradition says — traveling, righting wrongs, following a code — and who is finding that reality has more moral ambiguity than the code anticipated.
A ship's captain who lost a ship and who is quietly trying to get another one through a path that involves more risk than she'd prefer.
A cathedral canon who administers the practical affairs of a major religious institution and who has the financial and organizational knowledge of a senior executive.
A forest warden who has treaty authority over a specific area that two powerful factions both want to access and who intends to enforce the treaty regardless.
A retired assassin who has settled into domestic life and who is very good at domestic life and who will not discuss the retirement.
A bard who collects stories rather than performs them, who has been in a hundred significant events as a bystander, and who has developed a theory about how history actually works.
A miller who controls the only mill in a region of farmers and who is neither a villain nor a saint about this, just a person with leverage.
A tracker who finds things professionally and who has a personal policy about what she does when she finds them.
A minor noble whose family fortunes have declined to the point where the title is more weight than resource, navigating this with variable success.
A doctor who practices medicine in a city where medicine is still figuring itself out and who has committed to evidence in a field where evidence is contested.
A harbor pilot who guides ships through a difficult passage that has no written chart and that is dangerous enough that the pilot's fee is never negotiated.
A quartermaster who has been keeping an army fed, equipped, and positioned for six years and whose opinion of the generals is mixed.
A magical scholar who studies a specific narrow area of arcane theory and who is the foremost expert in the world in that narrow area and only that area.
A war orphan who grew up as a camp follower with an army and who has more practical military knowledge than most officers and no rank at all.
A steward who has managed a great house's affairs for forty years under three different lords and who has strong opinions about all three.
A traveling judge who rides circuit and who applies the law as written and who has seen enough of human nature to have expectations.
A spy master who appears to be something else entirely and who is here for reasons that the party may or may not intersect with.
An alchemist who has a legitimate business selling useful preparations and who funds the legitimate business with the less legitimate research that is their actual interest.
A ferryman on a river who has been crossing the same river for forty years and who has a view of what passes through a region that is unusual in its consistency.
A court herald who knows the genealogy of every noble house in the kingdom and whose access to this knowledge is coveted and occasionally dangerous.
A guard captain for a merchant caravan who is paid to be paranoid and who is, professionally, paranoid.
A former slave who bought their freedom and built a life afterward and who has specific opinions about freedom, obligation, and debt.
A clockmaker who has applied their precision to other problems — mechanical, logistical, occasionally interpersonal — with mixed but interesting results.
A street urchin who has been surviving in a city for eight years and who knows the city better than most of its inhabitants.
A temple archivist who has spent their career among records and who has, in the records, found things that were supposed to be lost.
A master of revels for a noble court who produces entertainment and who understands power, audience, and message better than most political advisors.
A retired thief-taker who worked the city watch's investigative branch and who now consults, occasionally, when cases don't resolve otherwise.
A witch who has been in this forest longer than the village it's adjacent to and who has watched the village do most of the things that villages do, with perspective.
A senior guild master in a trade that controls something essential, who holds this power with conscious awareness of its weight.
A squire who is ready to be a knight by every measure except the knight who is supposed to knight them, who is unavailable for reasons the squire hasn't been told.
A scholar of ancient languages who reads things no one else can and who considers this a responsibility rather than a privilege.
A wanderer who has been everywhere and who has nothing to prove about it and who will trade information freely for interesting conversation.
A bishop who administers a large diocese practically, politically, and with an awareness of the relationship between institutional power and moral authority.
A ranger who has been assigned a region that is more active than usual and who has been filing reports that are being ignored and who is now handling it personally.
A city planner who is trying to solve a problem that has no good solution — water, waste, fire, plague — with insufficient resources and constant political interference.
A dueling instructor who has trained a hundred students and who has opinions about which of them are good people and which are not, which doesn't always correlate with skill.
A customs official who is honest in a position where honesty is both more expensive and more politically complicated than corruption.
A traveling merchant who is also a spy — not exclusively either — who finds the combination professionally elegant.
A master armorer who makes armor for people who are going to be in serious danger and who has learned to care about who they're making it for.
A census taker who has spent five years counting and recording a kingdom's population and who knows where the soldiers, resources, and vulnerabilities are.
A high priest of a major deity who has stopped receiving divine guidance as clearly as they used to and who hasn't told anyone.
A former adventurer who retired with money, invested it, and now runs a business that serves adventurers and who hires people with that background specifically.
A royal advisor who has advised three monarchs and who has survived by being more useful than threatening, a calculation that requires constant maintenance.
A beekeeper who tends hives in a forest known for magical flora and who produces honey that does things that honey doesn't usually do.
A town crier who has been reading announcements for twenty years and who has developed a personal archive of everything that was officially true at some point.
A physician who has practiced long enough to see medicine change and who has updated their practice accordingly in a way that makes older colleagues uncomfortable.
A bridge warden who maintains a single bridge on a significant road and who knows every regular traveler and every irregular one.
A military engineer who has built fortifications in six kingdoms and who has very clear ideas about which fortifications will hold and which won't.
A young dragon that has decided to interact with human society more than is typical, for reasons that are unclear to everyone including the dragon.
A professional mourner who attends funerals for a fee and who has become genuinely skilled at grief — not performance of grief, but presence in it.
A harbor master who controls access to a port and who is neither corrupt nor naive about the relationship between commerce and authority.
A war correspondent who follows armies to record what happens and whose accounts are more accurate than official histories and considerably less popular with officials.
A tinkerer who fixes small things for small amounts of money and who has, in the course of fixing things, learned most of what there is to know about how things fail.
A herbalist who discovered something significant in their research and who is deciding whether to publish it, considering who will benefit and who will misuse it.
An explorer who has been somewhere no cartographer has been and who is now trying to get back to tell someone about it.
A prison warden who runs a facility justly by the standards of the institution and who has significant reservations about those standards.
A noble's personal secretary who runs the household, manages the correspondence, and has a more complete picture of the noble's affairs than the noble does.
A sailor who has been to the edge of the known map and seen what's there and who has a theory about what's beyond the edge and needs someone to fund finding out.
A former inquisitor who left the order because of a specific case and who has spent the years since working through the implications of that decision.
A river merchant who knows the trade routes, the currents, the seasonal variations, and the people at every stop on a five-hundred-mile stretch.
A golem keeper who maintains a collection of old automatons on behalf of a wizard who is no longer around to maintain them and who has begun to feel responsible for their wellbeing.
A ruined noble who has lost fortune but retained reputation and who is working out which of these is actually more useful.
A veteran cavalryman who became a horse breeder after the wars and who applies the same systematic intelligence to breeding that they applied to tactics.
A spy who has been in deep cover for so long that they have a genuine life on both sides, and whose loyalties have become genuinely complicated.
A village elder who has handled every significant event in this community for fifty years and who has developed a working theory of human nature from the evidence.
A traveling apothecary who serves remote communities on a seasonal circuit, whose knowledge of regional medical conditions is encyclopedic, and whose fees are sliding scale.
A professional adventurer who has been doing this for twenty years and who has the specific combination of competence and emotional detachment that comes from surviving that long in this field.
A person the party has heard of — a name that has come up in three separate contexts — who turns out to be not what any of those contexts suggested, but who is exactly what the situation requires.
